narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 63.9% chance that you would not pass. This prediction stems from a careful look at key elements of your profile, like your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, early-year and later-year school grade deciles, family income bracket, and enrollment plans. The model flagged your third-year grade decile of 1 as the biggest concern, since it placed you well below the typical range of around 6 for students who go on to pass the bar—strongly pushing the prediction toward expecting a failure. Your first-year decile of 2 added to that worry, similarly lagging behind the average of about 6 for successful bar takers and reinforcing the risk. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, while below the 3.24 average for those who pass, actually worked in your favor somewhat, nudging the prediction slightly toward success compared to even lower scores. Your LSAT score of 27, however, stood out as notably lower than the 37 average for passers, contributing to the overall risk picture alongside your upper-middle family income—which aligns with many who succeed—and your full-time enrollment plans, which are common among bar passers. Compared to typical accepted students, who often have stronger deciles and higher test scores, your profile showed patterns more aligned with those predicted to struggle on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven signal that these academic markers have historically been the strongest predictors of bar outcomes in our analysis. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or higher, and focus on improving your class rankings through targeted study habits or tutoring, as even modest gains in deciles can shift predictions dramatically. You might also explore part-time programs or gap-year experiences to build a stronger academic record. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact areas—reapplying after targeted improvements often leads to acceptance. Your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from an upper-middle background positions you well to turn this around.
